chemicals.concentration_notes: The toxicity of the Poinsettia is considered very mild, and actual poisoning is quite rare35. A cat would generally need to ingest a large amount of the plant to develop clinical signs1. When symptoms do occur, they are typically self-limiting and rarely require veterinary treatment1. However, one source notes that if a cat manages to eat a significantly large amount, it may suffer more serious sy
chemicals.description: in cats: The milky sap acts as a direct irritant to the mucous membranes2. When a cat bites into the leaves or stems, the sap irritates the mouth, esophagus, and gastrointestinal tract, which can lead to excessive drooling, nausea, vomiting, and diarrhea
chemicals.name: The sources identify the toxic substance simply as a "thick, milky, irritant sap

description:
Poinsettia (Euphorbia pulcherrima) is a common holiday plant with bright red, pink, or white bracts. It has a scary reputation, but for cats it is usually a mild irritant rather than a severe poison.

The main issue is the milky latex sap inside the leaves and stems. This sap contains irritating compounds, including diterpenoid euphorbol esters, that can bother the mouth, stomach, skin, or eyes. Most cats do not eat much because the plant tastes unpleasant, so serious poisoning is uncommon.

If a cat chews poinsettia, possible signs include drooling, mouth discomfort, vomiting, diarrhea, lower appetite, or low energy. Sap on the skin or in the eyes can cause redness or irritation. Keep poinsettias out of reach, wipe away fallen leaves, and contact a veterinarian if your cat eats a large amount, keeps vomiting, or has eye irritation.
emergencyNote: Gastric Lavage, Activated Charcoal, and Saline Cathartics
name: Poinsettia
safetyNotes: Main hazard: The milky latex sap in leaves and stems can irritate the mouth, stomach, skin, and eyes.
Symptoms: Drooling, mouth discomfort, vomiting, diarrhea, reduced appetite, or low energy; skin or eye contact may cause redness.
Risk level: Poinsettia is usually mildly irritating, not one of the highly dangerous holiday plants for cats.
Action: Keep the plant out of reach and call a vet if vomiting continues, eye irritation appears, or your cat ate a large amount.

symptoms.name: Oral irritation, excessive drooling (hypersalivation), and discomfort of the mouth and esophagus
symptoms.notes: This is caused by the thick, milky-white, latex sap found throughout the plant acting as a direct irritant to the mucous membranes1.... 2. Gastrointestinal Distress

symptoms.name: Vomiting, diarrhea, and nausea
symptoms.notes: Actual poisoning from a Poinsettia is considered rare; a cat generally needs to ingest a large amount of the plant to exhibit clinical signs56. The gastrointestinal symptoms are typically self-limiting and rarely require veterinary treatment6. 3. Anorexia

symptoms.name: Anorexia (loss of appetite)
symptoms.notes: This is usually secondary to the gastrointestinal irritation and nausea caused by the plant's sap2.... 4. Depression

symptoms.name: Depression or lethargy
symptoms.notes: This can present alongside the vomiting and anorexia if a significant amount of the plant is ingested6. 5. Cardiovascular Issues

symptoms.name: Cardiovascular issues
symptoms.notes: While Poinsettia toxicity is generally mild and limited to stomach upset, if a cat consumes a significantly large amount of the plant, it may suffer from more serious symptoms, including potential cardiovascular issues

treatments.description: For significant exposures, the veterinarian may pump or flush the stomach (gastric lavage) to remove the plant material. This can be followed by administering activated charcoal and a saline cathartic to bind the remaining toxins in the gastrointestinal tract and help them pass safely through the stool
treatments.name: Gastric Lavage, Activated Charcoal, and Saline Cathartics
treatments.notes: This is the most urgent medical intervention but should only be considered in specific cases, as a cat would generally need to ingest a significantly large amount of the plant to warrant this level of treatment23. 2. Resting the Gastrointestinal Tract

treatments.description: The veterinarian may recommend limiting the cat's food and water intake for a short period, typically one or two hours
treatments.name: Temporary Fasting (Withholding Food and Water)
treatments.notes: This is recommended if the cat is suspected of becoming sick or is actively vomiting after ingestion, as it gives the irritated gastrointestinal tract time to settle and recover2. 3. Symptomatic Care and Observation

treatments.description: The cat is simply monitored while the mild symptoms (such as drooling, vomiting, or diarrhea) naturally subside on their own
treatments.name: Observation and Symptomatic Support
treatments.notes: Because Poinsettia ingestion is generally self-limiting and not fatal, active treatment is rarely needed23. Most of the time, the irritation resolves completely on its own without requiring a clinic visit
